  6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
  7â†’
  8â†’import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
  9â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DatePeriodIso8601Serializer
 10â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer
 11â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DatePeriodComponentSerializer
 12â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DateTimePeriodComponentSerializer
 13â†’import kotlin.math.*
 14â†’import kotlin.time.Duration
 15â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 16â†’
 17â†’/**
 18â†’ * A difference between two [instants][Instant], decomposed into date and time components.
 19â†’ *
 20â†’ * The date components are: [years] ([DateTimeUnit.YEAR]), [months] ([DateTimeUnit.MONTH]), and [days] ([DateTimeUnit.DAY]).
 21â†’ *
 22â†’ * The time components are: [hours] ([DateTimeUnit.HOUR]), [minutes] ([DateTimeUnit.MINUTE]),
 23â†’ * [seconds] ([DateTimeUnit.SECOND]), and [nanoseconds] ([DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND]).
 24â†’ *
 25â†’ * The time components are not independent and are always normalized together.
 26â†’ * Likewise, months are normalized together with years.
 27â†’ * For example, there is no difference between `DateTimePeriod(months = 24, hours = 2, minutes = 63)` and
 28â†’ * `DateTimePeriod(years = 2, hours = 3, minutes = 3)`.
 29â†’ *
 30â†’ * All components can also be negative: for example, `DateTimePeriod(months = -5, days = 6, hours = -3)`.
 31â†’ * Whereas `months = 5` means "5 months after," `months = -5` means "5 months earlier."
 32â†’ *
 33â†’ * A constant time interval that consists of a single non-zero component (like "yearly" or "quarterly") should be
 34â†’ * represented by a [DateTimeUnit] directly instead of a [DateTimePeriod]:
 35â†’ * for example, instead of `DateTimePeriod(months = 6)`, one could use `DateTimeUnit.MONTH * 6`.
 36â†’ * This provides a wider variety of operations: for example, finding how many such intervals fit between two instants
 37â†’ * or dates or adding a multiple of such intervals at once.
 38â†’ *
 39â†’ * ### Interaction with other entities
 40â†’ *
 41â†’ * [DateTimePeriod] can be returned from [Instant.periodUntil], representing the difference between two instants.
 42â†’ * Conversely, there is an [Instant.plus] overload that accepts a [DateTimePeriod] and returns a new instant.
 43â†’ *
 44â†’ * [DatePeriod] is a subtype of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores the date components and has all time components equal
 45â†’ * to zero.
 46â†’ *
 47â†’ * [DateTimePeriod] can be thought of as a combination of a [Duration] and a [DatePeriod], as it contains both the
 48â†’ * time components of [Duration] and the date components of [DatePeriod].
 49â†’ * [Duration.toDateTimePeriod] can be used to convert a [Duration] to the corresponding [DateTimePeriod].
 50â†’ *
 51â†’ * ### Construction, serialization, and deserialization
 52â†’ *
 53â†’ * When a [DateTimePeriod] is constructed in any way, a [DatePeriod] value, which is a subtype of [DateTimePeriod],
 54â†’ * will be returned if all time components happen to be zero.
 55â†’ *
 56â†’ * A `DateTimePeriod` can be constructed using the constructor function with the same name.
 57â†’ * See sample 1.
 58â†’ *
 59â†’ * [parse] and [toString] methods can be used to obtain a [DateTimePeriod] from and convert it to a string in the
 60â†’ * ISO 8601 extended format.
 61â†’ * See sample 2.
 62â†’ *
 63â†’ * `DateTimePeriod` can also be returned as the result of instant arithmetic operations (see [Instant.periodUntil]).
 64â†’ *
 65â†’ * Additionally, there are several `kotlinx-serialization` serializers for [DateTimePeriod]:
 66â†’ * - [DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer] for the ISO 8601 format;
 67â†’ * - [DateTimePeriodComponentSerializer]  for an object with components.
 68â†’ *
 69â†’ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.construction
 70â†’ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.simpleParsingAndFormatting
 71â†’ */
 72â†’@Serializable(with = DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
 73â†’// TODO: could be error-prone without explicitly named params
 74â†’public sealed class DateTimePeriod {
 75â†’    internal abstract val totalMonths: Int
 76â†’
 77â†’    /**
 78â†’     * The number of calendar days. Can be negative.
 79â†’     *
 80â†’     * Note that a calendar day is not identical to 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
 81â†’     * Also, this field does not get normalized together with months, so values larger than 31 can be present.
 82â†’     *
 83â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 84â†’     */
 85â†’    public abstract val days: Int
 86â†’    internal abstract val totalNanoseconds: Long
 87â†’
 88â†’    /**
 89â†’     * The number of whole years. Can be negative.
 90â†’     *
 91â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 92â†’     */
 93â†’    public val years: Int get() = totalMonths / 12
 94â†’
 95â†’    /**
 96â†’     * The number of months in this period that don't form a whole year, so this value is always in `(-11..11)`.
 97â†’     *
 98â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 99â†’     */
100â†’    public val months: Int get() = totalMonths % 12
101â†’
102â†’    /**
103â†’     * The number of whole hours in this period. Can be negative.
104â†’     *
105â†’     * This field does not get normalized together with days, so values larger than 23 or smaller than -23 can be present.
106â†’     *
107â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
108â†’     */
109â†’    public open val hours: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds / 3_600_000_000_000).toInt()
110â†’
111â†’    /**
112â†’     * The number of whole minutes in this period that don't form a whole hour, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
113â†’     *
114â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
115â†’     */
116â†’    public open val minutes: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 3_600_000_000_000) / 60_000_000_000).toInt()
117â†’
118â†’    /**
119â†’     * The number of whole seconds in this period that don't form a whole minute, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
120â†’     *
121â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
122â†’     */
123â†’    public open val seconds: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 60_000_000_000) / NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
124â†’
125â†’    /**
126â†’     * The number of whole nanoseconds in this period that don't form a whole second, so this value is always in
127â†’     * `(-999_999_999..999_999_999)`.
128â†’     *
129â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
130â†’     */
131â†’    public open val nanoseconds: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
132â†’
133â†’    private fun allNonpositive() =
134â†’        totalMonths <= 0 && days <= 0 && totalNanoseconds <= 0 && (totalMonths or days != 0 || totalNanoseconds != 0L)
135â†’
136â†’    /**
137â†’     * Converts this period to the ISO 8601 string representation for durations, for example, `P2M1DT3H`.
138â†’     *
139â†’     * Note that the ISO 8601 duration is not the same as [Duration],
140â†’     * but instead includes the date components, like [DateTimePeriod] does.
141â†’     *
142â†’     * Examples of the output:
143â†’     * - `P2Y4M-1D`: two years, four months, minus one day;
144â†’     * - `-P2Y4M1D`: minus two years, minus four months, minus one day;
145â†’     * - `P1DT3H2M4.123456789S`: one day, three hours, two minutes, four seconds, 123456789 nanoseconds;
146â†’     * - `P1DT-3H-2M-4.123456789S`: one day, minus three hours, minus two minutes,
147â†’     *   minus four seconds, minus 123456789 nanoseconds;
148â†’     *
149â†’     * See ISO-8601-1:2019, 5.5.2.2a)
150â†’     *
151â†’     * @see DateTimePeriod.parse for the detailed description of the format.
152â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.toStringSample
153â†’     */
154â†’    override fun toString(): String = buildString {
155â†’        val sign = if (allNonpositive()) { append('-'); -1 } else 1
156â†’        append('P')
157â†’        if (years != 0) append(years * sign).append('Y')
158â†’        if (months != 0) append(months * sign).append('M')
159â†’        if (days != 0) append(days * sign).append('D')
160â†’        var t = "T"
161â†’        if (hours != 0) append(t).append(hours * sign).append('H').also { t = "" }
162â†’        if (minutes != 0) append(t).append(minutes * sign).append('M').also { t = "" }
163â†’        if (seconds or nanoseconds != 0) {
164â†’            append(t)
165â†’            append(when {
166â†’                seconds != 0 -> seconds * sign
167â†’                nanoseconds * sign < 0 -> "-0"
168â†’                else -> "0"
169â†’            })
170â†’            if (nanoseconds != 0) append('.').append((nanoseconds.absoluteValue).toString().padStart(9, '0'))
171â†’            append('S')
172â†’        }
173â†’
174â†’        if (length == 1) append("0D")
175â†’    }
176â†’
177â†’    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
178â†’        if (this === other) return true
179â†’        if (other !is DateTimePeriod) return false
180â†’
181â†’        if (totalMonths != other.totalMonths) return false
182â†’        if (days != other.days) return false
183â†’        if (totalNanoseconds != other.totalNanoseconds) return false
184â†’
185â†’        return true
186â†’    }
187â†’
188â†’    override fun hashCode(): Int {
189â†’        var result = totalMonths
190â†’        result = 31 * result + days
191â†’        result = 31 * result + totalNanoseconds.hashCode()
192â†’        return result
193â†’    }
194â†’
195â†’    public companion object {
196â†’        /**
197â†’         * Parses a ISO 8601 duration string as a [DateTimePeriod].
198â†’         *
199â†’         * If the time components are absent or equal to zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
200â†’         *
201â†’         * Note that the ISO 8601 duration is not the same as [Duration],
202â†’         * but instead includes the date components, like [DateTimePeriod] does.
203â†’         *
204â†’         * Examples of durations in the ISO 8601 format:
205â†’         * - `P1Y40D` is one year and 40 days
206â†’         * - `-P1DT1H` is minus (one day and one hour)
207â†’         * - `P1DT-1H` is one day minus one hour
208â†’         * - `-PT0.000000001S` is minus one nanosecond
209â†’         *
210â†’         * The format is defined as follows:
211â†’         * - First, optionally, a `-` or `+`.
212â†’         *   If `-` is present, the whole period after the `-` is negated: `-P-2M1D` is the same as `P2M-1D`.
213â†’         * - Then, the letter `P`.
214â†’         * - Optionally, the number of years, followed by `Y`.
215â†’         * - Optionally, the number of months, followed by `M`.
216â†’         * - Optionally, the number of weeks, followed by `W`.
217â†’         * - Optionally, the number of days, followed by `D`.
218â†’         * - The string can end here if there are no more time components.
219â†’         *   If there are time components, the letter `T` is required.
220â†’         * - Optionally, the number of hours, followed by `H`.
221â†’         * - Optionally, the number of minutes, followed by `M`.
222â†’         * - Optionally, the number of seconds, followed by `S`.
223â†’         *   Seconds can optionally have a fractional part with up to nine digits.
224â†’         *   The fractional part is separated with a `.`.
225â†’         *
226â†’         * An explicit `+` or `-` sign can be prepended to any number.
227â†’         * `-` means that the number is negative, and `+` has no effect.
228â†’         *
229â†’         * See ISO-8601-1:2019, 5.5.2.2a) and 5.5.2.2b).
230â†’         * We combine the two formats into one by allowing the number of weeks to go after the number of months
231â†’         * and before the number of days.
232â†’         *
233â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [DateTimePeriod] are
234â†’         * exceeded.
235â†’         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.parsing
236â†’         */
237â†’        public fun parse(text: String): DateTimePeriod {
238â†’            fun parseException(message: String, position: Int): Nothing =
239â†’                throw DateTimeFormatException("Parse error at char $position: $message")
240â†’            val START = 0
241â†’            val AFTER_P = 1
242â†’            val AFTER_YEAR = 2
243â†’            val AFTER_MONTH = 3
244â†’            val AFTER_WEEK = 4
245â†’            val AFTER_DAY = 5
246â†’            val AFTER_T = 6
247â†’            val AFTER_HOUR = 7
248â†’            val AFTER_MINUTE = 8
249â†’            val AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO = 9
250â†’
251â†’            var state = START
252â†’            // next unread character
253â†’            var i = 0
254â†’            var sign = 1
255â†’            var years = 0
256â†’            var months = 0
257â†’            var weeks = 0
258â†’            var days = 0
259â†’            var hours = 0
260â†’            var minutes = 0
261â†’            var seconds = 0
262â†’            var nanoseconds = 0
263â†’            while (true) {
264â†’                if (i >= text.length) {
265â†’                    if (state == START)
266â†’                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; 'P' designator is required", i)
267â†’                    if (state == AFTER_T)
268â†’                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; at least one time component is required after 'T'", i)
269â†’                    val daysTotal = when (val n = days.toLong() + weeks * 7) {
270â†’                        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> n.toInt()
271â†’                        else -> parseException("The total number of days under 'D' and 'W' designators should fit into an Int", 0)
272â†’                    }
273â†’                    return DateTimePeriod(years, months, daysTotal, hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds.toLong())
274â†’                }
275â†’                if (state == START) {
276â†’                    if (i + 1 >= text.length && (text[i] == '+' || text[i] == '-'))
277â†’                        parseException("Unexpected end of string; 'P' designator is required", i)
278â†’                    when (text[i]) {
279â†’                        '+', '-' -> {
280â†’                            if (text[i] == '-')
281â†’                                sign = -1
282â†’                            if (text[i + 1] != 'P')
283â†’                                parseException("Expected 'P', got '${text[i + 1]}'", i + 1)
284â†’                            i += 2
285â†’                        }
286â†’                        'P' -> { i += 1 }
287â†’                        else -> parseException("Expected '+', '-', 'P', got '${text[i]}'", i)
288â†’                    }
289â†’                    state = AFTER_P
290â†’                    continue
291â†’                }
292â†’                var localSign = sign
293â†’                val iStart = i
294â†’                when (text[i]) {
295â†’                    '+', '-' -> {
296â†’                        if (text[i] == '-') localSign *= -1
297â†’                        i += 1
298â†’                        if (i >= text.length || text[i] !in '0'..'9')
299â†’                            parseException("A number expected after '${text[i]}'", i)
300â†’                    }
301â†’                    in '0'..'9' -> { }
302â†’                    'T' -> {
303â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_T)
304â†’                            parseException("Only one 'T' designator is allowed", i)
305â†’                        state = AFTER_T
306â†’                        i += 1
307â†’                        continue
308â†’                    }
309â†’                }
310â†’                var number = 0L
311â†’                while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9') {
312â†’                    try {
313â†’                        number = safeAdd(safeMultiply(number, 10), (text[i] - '0').toLong())
314â†’                    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
315â†’                        parseException("The number is too large", iStart)
316â†’                    }
317â†’                    i += 1
318â†’                }
319â†’                number *= localSign
320â†’                if (i == text.length)
321â†’                    parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
322â†’                val wrongOrder = "Wrong component order: should be 'Y', 'M', 'W', 'D', then designator 'T', then 'H', 'M', 'S'"
323â†’                fun Long.toIntThrowing(component: Char): Int {
324â†’                    if (this < Int.MIN_VALUE || this > Int.MAX_VALUE)
325â†’                        parseException("Value $this does not fit into an Int, which is required for component '$component'", iStart)
326â†’                    return toInt()
327â†’                }
328â†’                when (text[i].uppercaseChar()) {
329â†’                    'Y' -> {
330â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_YEAR)
331â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
332â†’                        state = AFTER_YEAR
333â†’                        years = number.toIntThrowing('Y')
334â†’                    }
335â†’                    'M' -> {
336â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_T) {
337â†’                            // Minutes
338â†’                            if (state >= AFTER_MINUTE)
339â†’                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
340â†’                            state = AFTER_MINUTE
341â†’                            minutes = number.toIntThrowing('M')
342â†’                        } else {
343â†’                            // Months
344â†’                            if (state >= AFTER_MONTH)
345â†’                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
346â†’                            state = AFTER_MONTH
347â†’                            months = number.toIntThrowing('M')
348â†’                        }
349â†’                    }
350â†’                    'W' -> {
351â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_WEEK)
352â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
353â†’                        state = AFTER_WEEK
354â†’                        weeks = number.toIntThrowing('W')
355â†’                    }
356â†’                    'D' -> {
357â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_DAY)
358â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
359â†’                        state = AFTER_DAY
360â†’                        days = number.toIntThrowing('D')
361â†’                    }
362â†’                    'H' -> {
363â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_HOUR || state < AFTER_T)
364â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
365â†’                        state = AFTER_HOUR
366â†’                        hours = number.toIntThrowing('H')
367â†’                    }
368â†’                    'S' -> {
369â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
370â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
371â†’                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
372â†’                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
373â†’                    }
374â†’                    '.', ',' -> {
375â†’                        i += 1
376â†’                        if (i >= text.length)
377â†’                            parseException("Expected designator 'S' after ${text[i - 1]}", i)
378â†’                        val iStartFraction = i
379â†’                        while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9')
380â†’                            i += 1
381â†’                        val fractionLength = i - iStartFraction
382â†’                        if (fractionLength > 9)
383â†’                            parseException("Only the nanosecond fractions of a second are supported", iStartFraction)
384â†’                        val fractionalPart = text.substring(iStartFraction, i) + "0".repeat(9 - fractionLength)
385â†’                        nanoseconds = fractionalPart.toInt(10) * localSign
386â†’                        if (text[i] != 'S')
387â†’                            parseException("Expected the 'S' designator after a fraction", i)
388â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
389â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
390â†’                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
391â†’                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
392â†’                    }
393â†’                    else -> parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
394â†’                }
395â†’                i += 1
396â†’           }
397â†’        }
398â†’    }
399â†’}

402â†’ * @suppress
403â†’ */
404â†’@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DateTimePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
405â†’public fun String.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = DateTimePeriod.parse(this)

407â†’/**
408â†’ * A special case of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores the date components and has all time components equal to zero.
409â†’ *
410â†’ * A `DatePeriod` is automatically returned from all constructor functions for [DateTimePeriod] if it turns out that
411â†’ * the time components are zero.
412â†’ *
413â†’ * ```
414â†’ * DateTimePeriod.parse("P1Y3D") as DatePeriod // 1 year and 3 days
415â†’ * ```
416â†’ *
417â†’ * Additionally, [DatePeriod] has its own constructor, the [parse] function that will fail if any of the time components
418â†’ * are not zero, and [DatePeriodIso8601Serializer] and [DatePeriodComponentSerializer], mirroring those of
419â†’ * [DateTimePeriod].
420â†’ *
421â†’ * `DatePeriod` values are used in operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate] and are returned from operations
422â†’ * on [LocalDates][LocalDate], but they also can be passed anywhere a [DateTimePeriod] is expected.
423â†’ *
424â†’ * On the JVM, there are `DatePeriod.toJavaPeriod()` and `java.time.Period.toKotlinDatePeriod()`
425â†’ * extension functions to convert between `kotlinx.datetime` and `java.time` objects used for the same purpose.
426â†’ *
427â†’ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DatePeriodSamples.simpleParsingAndFormatting
428â†’ */
429â†’@Serializable(with = DatePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
430â†’public class DatePeriod internal constructor(
431â†’    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
432â†’    override val days: Int,
433â†’) : DateTimePeriod() {
434â†’    /**
435â†’     * Constructs a new [DatePeriod].
436â†’     *
437â†’     * It is recommended to always name the arguments explicitly when constructing this manually,
438â†’     * like `DatePeriod(years = 1, months = 12, days = 16)`.
439â†’     *
440â†’     * The passed numbers are not stored as is but are normalized instead for human readability, so, for example,
441â†’     * `DateTimePeriod(months = 24, days = 41)` becomes `DateTimePeriod(years = 2, days = 41)`.
442â†’     *
443â†’     * If only a single component is set and is always non-zero and is semantically a fixed time interval
444â†’     * (like "yearly" or "quarterly"), please consider using a multiple of [DateTimeUnit.DateBased] instead.
445â†’     * For example, instead of `DatePeriod(months = 6)`, one can use `DateTimeUnit.MONTH * 6`.
446â†’     *
447â†’     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [years] and [months] overflows an [Int].
448â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DatePeriodSamples.construction
449â†’     */
450â†’    public constructor(years: Int = 0, months: Int = 0, days: Int = 0): this(totalMonths(years, months), days)
451â†’    // avoiding excessive computations
452â†’    /** The number of whole hours in this period. Always equal to zero. */
453â†’    override val hours: Int get() = 0
454â†’
455â†’    /** The number of whole minutes in this period. Always equal to zero. */
456â†’    override val minutes: Int get() = 0
457â†’
458â†’    /** The number of whole seconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
459â†’    override val seconds: Int get() = 0
460â†’
461â†’    /** The number of nanoseconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
462â†’    override val nanoseconds: Int get() = 0
463â†’    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long get() = 0
464â†’
465â†’    public companion object {
466â†’        /**
467â†’         * Parses the ISO 8601 duration representation as a [DatePeriod], for example, `P1Y2M30D`.
468â†’         *
469â†’         * This function is equivalent to [DateTimePeriod.parse], but will fail if any of the time components are not
470â†’         * zero.
471â†’         *
472â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed, the boundaries of [DatePeriod] are exceeded,
473â†’         * or any time components are not zero.
474â†’         *
475â†’         * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
476â†’         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DatePeriodSamples.parsing
477â†’         */
478â†’        public fun parse(text: String): DatePeriod =
479â†’            when (val period = DateTimePeriod.parse(text)) {
480â†’                is DatePeriod -> period
481â†’                else -> throw DateTimeFormatException("Period $period (parsed from string $text) is not date-based")
482â†’            }
483â†’    }
484â†’}
485â†’
486â†’/**
487â†’ * @suppress
488â†’ */
489â†’@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DatePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
490â†’public fun String.toDatePeriod(): DatePeriod = DatePeriod.parse(this)
491â†’
492â†’private class DateTimePeriodImpl(
493â†’    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
494â†’    override val days: Int,
495â†’    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long,
496â†’) : DateTimePeriod()
497â†’
498â†’// TODO: these calculations fit in a JS Number. Possible to do an expect/actual here.
499â†’private fun totalMonths(years: Int, months: Int): Int =
500â†’    when (val totalMonths = years.toLong() * 12 + months.toLong()) {
501â†’        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> totalMonths.toInt()
502â†’        else -> throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of months in $years years and $months months overflows an Int")
503â†’    }
504â†’
505â†’private fun totalNanoseconds(hours: Int, minutes: Int, seconds: Int, nanoseconds: Long): Long {
506â†’    val totalMinutes: Long = hours.toLong() * 60 + minutes
507â†’    // absolute value at most 61 * Int.MAX_VALUE
508â†’    val totalMinutesAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutes * 60
509â†’    // absolute value at most 61 * 60 * Int.MAX_VALUE < 64 * 64 * 2^31 = 2^43
510â†’    val minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutesAsSeconds + nanoseconds / NANOS_PER_ONE
511â†’    // absolute value at most 2^43 + 2^63 / 10^9 < 2^43 + 2^34 < 2^44
512â†’    val totalSeconds = minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds + seconds
513â†’    // absolute value at most 2^44 + 2^31 < 2^45
514â†’    return try {
515â†’        multiplyAndAdd(totalSeconds, 1_000_000_000, nanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE)
516â†’    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
517â†’        throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of nanoseconds in $hours hours, $minutes minutes, $seconds seconds, and $nanoseconds nanoseconds overflows a Long")
518â†’    }
519â†’}
520â†’
521â†’internal fun buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths: Int = 0, days: Int = 0, totalNanoseconds: Long): DateTimePeriod =
522â†’    if (totalNanoseconds != 0L)
523â†’        DateTimePeriodImpl(totalMonths, days, totalNanoseconds)
524â†’    else
525â†’        DatePeriod(totalMonths, days)
526â†’
527â†’/**
528â†’ * Constructs a new [DateTimePeriod]. If all the time components are zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
529â†’ *
530â†’ * It is recommended to always name the arguments explicitly when constructing this manually,
531â†’ * like `DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 12, days = 16)`.
532â†’ *
533â†’ * The passed numbers are not stored as is but are normalized instead for human readability, so, for example,
534â†’ * `DateTimePeriod(months = 24, days = 41)` becomes `DateTimePeriod(years = 2, days = 41)`.
535â†’ *
536â†’ * If only a single component is set and is always non-zero and is semantically a fixed time interval
537â†’ * (like "yearly" or "quarterly"), please consider using a multiple of [DateTimeUnit] instead.
538â†’ * For example, instead of `DateTimePeriod(months = 6)`, one can use `DateTimeUnit.MONTH * 6`.
539â†’ *
540â†’ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [years] and [months] overflows an [Int].
541â†’ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [hours], [minutes], [seconds] and [nanoseconds]
542â†’ * overflows a [Long].
543â†’ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.constructorFunction
544â†’ */
545â†’public fun DateTimePeriod(
546â†’    years: Int = 0,
547â†’    months: Int = 0,
548â†’    days: Int = 0,
549â†’    hours: Int = 0,
550â†’    minutes: Int = 0,
551â†’    seconds: Int = 0,
552â†’    nanoseconds: Long = 0
553â†’): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths(years, months), days,
554â†’    totalNanoseconds(hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds))
555â†’
556â†’/**
557â†’ * Constructs a [DateTimePeriod] from a [Duration].
558â†’ *
559â†’ * If the duration value is too big to be represented as a [Long] number of nanoseconds,
560â†’ * the result will be [Long.MAX_VALUE] nanoseconds.
561â†’ *
562â†’ * **Pitfall**: a [DateTimePeriod] obtained this way will always have zero date components.
563â†’ * The reason is that even a [Duration] obtained via [Duration.Companion.days] just means a multiple of 24 hours,
564â†’ * whereas in `kotlinx-datetime`, a day is a calendar day, which can be different from 24 hours.
565â†’ * See [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
566â†’ *
567â†’ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.durationToDateTimePeriod
568â†’ */
569â†’// TODO: maybe it's more consistent to throw here on overflow?
570â†’public fun Duration.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalNanoseconds = inWholeNanoseconds)
571â†’
572â†’/**
573â†’ * Adds two [DateTimePeriod] instances.
574â†’ *
575â†’ * **Pitfall**: given three instants, adding together the periods between the first and the second and between the
576â†’ * second and the third *does not* necessarily equal the period between the first and the third.
577â†’ *
578â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
579â†’ */
580â†’public operator fun DateTimePeriod.plus(other: DateTimePeriod): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(
581â†’    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
582â†’    safeAdd(days, other.days),
583â†’    safeAdd(totalNanoseconds, other.totalNanoseconds),
584â†’)
585â†’
586â†’/**
587â†’ * Adds two [DatePeriod] instances.
588â†’ *
589â†’ * **Pitfall**: given three dates, adding together the periods between the first and the second and between the
590â†’ * second and the third *does not* necessarily equal the period between the first and the third.
591â†’ *
592â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
593â†’ */
594â†’public operator fun DatePeriod.plus(other: DatePeriod): DatePeriod = DatePeriod(
595â†’    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
596â†’    safeAdd(days, other.days),
597â†’)
598â†’

1â†’/*
  2â†’ * Copyright 2019-2020 JetBrains s.r.o.

  4â†’ */
  5â†’
  6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
  7â†’
  8â†’import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
  9â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DatePeriodIso8601Serializer
 10â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer
 11â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DatePeriodComponentSerializer
 12â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DateTimePeriodComponentSerializer
 13â†’import kotlin.math.*
 14â†’import kotlin.time.Duration
 15â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 16â†’
 17â†’/**
 18â†’ * A difference between two [instants][Instant], decomposed into date and time components.
 19â†’ *
 20â†’ * The date components are: [years] ([DateTimeUnit.YEAR]), [months] ([DateTimeUnit.MONTH]), and [days] ([DateTimeUnit.DAY]).
 21â†’ *
 22â†’ * The time components are: [hours] ([DateTimeUnit.HOUR]), [minutes] ([DateTimeUnit.MINUTE]),
 23â†’ * [seconds] ([DateTimeUnit.SECOND]), and [nanoseconds] ([DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND]).
 24â†’ *
 25â†’ * The time components are not independent and are always normalized together.
 26â†’ * Likewise, months are normalized together with years.
 27â†’ * For example, there is no difference between `DateTimePeriod(months = 24, hours = 2, minutes = 63)` and
 28â†’ * `DateTimePeriod(years = 2, hours = 3, minutes = 3)`.
 29â†’ *
 30â†’ * All components can also be negative: for example, `DateTimePeriod(months = -5, days = 6, hours = -3)`.
 31â†’ * Whereas `months = 5` means "5 months after," `months = -5` means "5 months earlier."
 32â†’ *
 33â†’ * A constant time interval that consists of a single non-zero component (like "yearly" or "quarterly") should be
 34â†’ * represented by a [DateTimeUnit] directly instead of a [DateTimePeriod]:
 35â†’ * for example, instead of `DateTimePeriod(months = 6)`, one could use `DateTimeUnit.MONTH * 6`.
 36â†’ * This provides a wider variety of operations: for example, finding how many such intervals fit between two instants
 37â†’ * or dates or adding a multiple of such intervals at once.
 38â†’ *
 39â†’ * ### Interaction with other entities
 40â†’ *
 41â†’ * [DateTimePeriod] can be returned from [Instant.periodUntil], representing the difference between two instants.
 42â†’ * Conversely, there is an [Instant.plus] overload that accepts a [DateTimePeriod] and returns a new instant.
 43â†’ *
 44â†’ * [DatePeriod] is a subtype of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores the date components and has all time components equal
 45â†’ * to zero.
 46â†’ *
 47â†’ * [DateTimePeriod] can be thought of as a combination of a [Duration] and a [DatePeriod], as it contains both the
 48â†’ * time components of [Duration] and the date components of [DatePeriod].
 49â†’ * [Duration.toDateTimePeriod] can be used to convert a [Duration] to the corresponding [DateTimePeriod].
 50â†’ *
 51â†’ * ### Construction, serialization, and deserialization
 52â†’ *
 53â†’ * When a [DateTimePeriod] is constructed in any way, a [DatePeriod] value, which is a subtype of [DateTimePeriod],
 54â†’ * will be returned if all time components happen to be zero.
 55â†’ *
 56â†’ * A `DateTimePeriod` can be constructed using the constructor function with the same name.
 57â†’ * See sample 1.
 58â†’ *
 59â†’ * [parse] and [toString] methods can be used to obtain a [DateTimePeriod] from and convert it to a string in the
 60â†’ * ISO 8601 extended format.
 61â†’ * See sample 2.
 62â†’ *
 63â†’ * `DateTimePeriod` can also be returned as the result of instant arithmetic operations (see [Instant.periodUntil]).
 64â†’ *
 65â†’ * Additionally, there are several `kotlinx-serialization` serializers for [DateTimePeriod]:
 66â†’ * - [DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer] for the ISO 8601 format;
 67â†’ * - [DateTimePeriodComponentSerializer]  for an object with components.
 68â†’ *
 69â†’ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.construction
 70â†’ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.simpleParsingAndFormatting
 71â†’ */
 72â†’@Serializable(with = DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
 73â†’// TODO: could be error-prone without explicitly named params
 74â†’public sealed class DateTimePeriod {
 75â†’    internal abstract val totalMonths: Int
 76â†’
 77â†’    /**
 78â†’     * The number of calendar days. Can be negative.
 79â†’     *
 80â†’     * Note that a calendar day is not identical to 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
 81â†’     * Also, this field does not get normalized together with months, so values larger than 31 can be present.
 82â†’     *
 83â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 84â†’     */
 85â†’    public abstract val days: Int
 86â†’    internal abstract val totalNanoseconds: Long
 87â†’
 88â†’    /**
 89â†’     * The number of whole years. Can be negative.
 90â†’     *
 91â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 92â†’     */
 93â†’    public val years: Int get() = totalMonths / 12
 94â†’
 95â†’    /**
 96â†’     * The number of months in this period that don't form a whole year, so this value is always in `(-11..11)`.
 97â†’     *
 98â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 99â†’     */
100â†’    public val months: Int get() = totalMonths % 12
101â†’
102â†’    /**
103â†’     * The number of whole hours in this period. Can be negative.
104â†’     *
105â†’     * This field does not get normalized together with days, so values larger than 23 or smaller than -23 can be present.
106â†’     *
107â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
108â†’     */
109â†’    public open val hours: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds / 3_600_000_000_000).toInt()
110â†’
111â†’    /**
112â†’     * The number of whole minutes in this period that don't form a whole hour, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
113â†’     *
114â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
115â†’     */
116â†’    public open val minutes: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 3_600_000_000_000) / 60_000_000_000).toInt()
117â†’
118â†’    /**
119â†’     * The number of whole seconds in this period that don't form a whole minute, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
120â†’     *
121â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
122â†’     */
123â†’    public open val seconds: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 60_000_000_000) / NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
124â†’
125â†’    /**
126â†’     * The number of whole nanoseconds in this period that don't form a whole second, so this value is always in
127â†’     * `(-999_999_999..999_999_999)`.
128â†’     *
129â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
130â†’     */
131â†’    public open val nanoseconds: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
132â†’
133â†’    private fun allNonpositive() =
134â†’        totalMonths <= 0 && days <= 0 && totalNanoseconds <= 0 && (totalMonths or days != 0 || totalNanoseconds != 0L)
135â†’
136â†’    /**
137â†’     * Converts this period to the ISO 8601 string representation for durations, for example, `P2M1DT3H`.
138â†’     *
139â†’     * Note that the ISO 8601 duration is not the same as [Duration],
140â†’     * but instead includes the date components, like [DateTimePeriod] does.
141â†’     *
142â†’     * Examples of the output:
143â†’     * - `P2Y4M-1D`: two years, four months, minus one day;
144â†’     * - `-P2Y4M1D`: minus two years, minus four months, minus one day;
145â†’     * - `P1DT3H2M4.123456789S`: one day, three hours, two minutes, four seconds, 123456789 nanoseconds;
146â†’     * - `P1DT-3H-2M-4.123456789S`: one day, minus three hours, minus two minutes,
147â†’     *   minus four seconds, minus 123456789 nanoseconds;
148â†’     *
149â†’     * See ISO-8601-1:2019, 5.5.2.2a)
150â†’     *
151â†’     * @see DateTimePeriod.parse for the detailed description of the format.
152â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.toStringSample
153â†’     */
154â†’    override fun toString(): String = buildString {
155â†’        val sign = if (allNonpositive()) { append('-'); -1 } else 1
156â†’        append('P')
157â†’        if (years != 0) append(years * sign).append('Y')
158â†’        if (months != 0) append(months * sign).append('M')
159â†’        if (days != 0) append(days * sign).append('D')
160â†’        var t = "T"
161â†’        if (hours != 0) append(t).append(hours * sign).append('H').also { t = "" }
162â†’        if (minutes != 0) append(t).append(minutes * sign).append('M').also { t = "" }
163â†’        if (seconds or nanoseconds != 0) {
164â†’            append(t)
165â†’            append(when {
166â†’                seconds != 0 -> seconds * sign
167â†’                nanoseconds * sign < 0 -> "-0"
168â†’                else -> "0"
169â†’            })
170â†’            if (nanoseconds != 0) append('.').append((nanoseconds.absoluteValue).toString().padStart(9, '0'))
171â†’            append('S')
172â†’        }
173â†’
174â†’        if (length == 1) append("0D")
175â†’    }
176â†’
177â†’    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
178â†’        if (this === other) return true
179â†’        if (other !is DateTimePeriod) return false
180â†’
181â†’        if (totalMonths != other.totalMonths) return false
182â†’        if (days != other.days) return false
183â†’        if (totalNanoseconds != other.totalNanoseconds) return false
184â†’
185â†’        return true
186â†’    }
187â†’
188â†’    override fun hashCode(): Int {
189â†’        var result = totalMonths
190â†’        result = 31 * result + days
191â†’        result = 31 * result + totalNanoseconds.hashCode()
192â†’        return result
193â†’    }
194â†’
195â†’    public companion object {
196â†’        /**
197â†’         * Parses a ISO 8601 duration string as a [DateTimePeriod].
198â†’         *
199â†’         * If the time components are absent or equal to zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
200â†’         *
201â†’         * Note that the ISO 8601 duration is not the same as [Duration],
202â†’         * but instead includes the date components, like [DateTimePeriod] does.
203â†’         *
204â†’         * Examples of durations in the ISO 8601 format:
205â†’         * - `P1Y40D` is one year and 40 days
206â†’         * - `-P1DT1H` is minus (one day and one hour)
207â†’         * - `P1DT-1H` is one day minus one hour
208â†’         * - `-PT0.000000001S` is minus one nanosecond
209â†’         *
210â†’         * The format is defined as follows:
211â†’         * - First, optionally, a `-` or `+`.
212â†’         *   If `-` is present, the whole period after the `-` is negated: `-P-2M1D` is the same as `P2M-1D`.
213â†’         * - Then, the letter `P`.
214â†’         * - Optionally, the number of years, followed by `Y`.
215â†’         * - Optionally, the number of months, followed by `M`.
216â†’         * - Optionally, the number of weeks, followed by `W`.
217â†’         * - Optionally, the number of days, followed by `D`.
218â†’         * - The string can end here if there are no more time components.
219â†’         *   If there are time components, the letter `T` is required.
220â†’         * - Optionally, the number of hours, followed by `H`.
221â†’         * - Optionally, the number of minutes, followed by `M`.
222â†’         * - Optionally, the number of seconds, followed by `S`.
223â†’         *   Seconds can optionally have a fractional part with up to nine digits.
224â†’         *   The fractional part is separated with a `.`.
225â†’         *
226â†’         * An explicit `+` or `-` sign can be prepended to any number.
227â†’         * `-` means that the number is negative, and `+` has no effect.
228â†’         *
229â†’         * See ISO-8601-1:2019, 5.5.2.2a) and 5.5.2.2b).
230â†’         * We combine the two formats into one by allowing the number of weeks to go after the number of months
231â†’         * and before the number of days.
232â†’         *
233â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [DateTimePeriod] are
234â†’         * exceeded.
235â†’         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.parsing
236â†’         */
237â†’        public fun parse(text: String): DateTimePeriod {
238â†’            fun parseException(message: String, position: Int): Nothing =
239â†’                throw DateTimeFormatException("Parse error at char $position: $message")
240â†’            val START = 0
241â†’            val AFTER_P = 1
242â†’            val AFTER_YEAR = 2
243â†’            val AFTER_MONTH = 3
244â†’            val AFTER_WEEK = 4
245â†’            val AFTER_DAY = 5
246â†’            val AFTER_T = 6
247â†’            val AFTER_HOUR = 7
248â†’            val AFTER_MINUTE = 8
249â†’            val AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO = 9
250â†’
251â†’            var state = START
252â†’            // next unread character
253â†’            var i = 0
254â†’            var sign = 1
255â†’            var years = 0
256â†’            var months = 0
257â†’            var weeks = 0
258â†’            var days = 0
259â†’            var hours = 0
260â†’            var minutes = 0
261â†’            var seconds = 0
262â†’            var nanoseconds = 0
263â†’            while (true) {
264â†’                if (i >= text.length) {
265â†’                    if (state == START)
266â†’                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; 'P' designator is required", i)
267â†’                    if (state == AFTER_T)
268â†’                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; at least one time component is required after 'T'", i)
269â†’                    val daysTotal = when (val n = days.toLong() + weeks * 7) {
270â†’                        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> n.toInt()
271â†’                        else -> parseException("The total number of days under 'D' and 'W' designators should fit into an Int", 0)
272â†’                    }
273â†’                    return DateTimePeriod(years, months, daysTotal, hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds.toLong())
274â†’                }
275â†’                if (state == START) {
276â†’                    if (i + 1 >= text.length && (text[i] == '+' || text[i] == '-'))
277â†’                        parseException("Unexpected end of string; 'P' designator is required", i)
278â†’                    when (text[i]) {
279â†’                        '+', '-' -> {
280â†’                            if (text[i] == '-')
281â†’                                sign = -1
282â†’                            if (text[i + 1] != 'P')
283â†’                                parseException("Expected 'P', got '${text[i + 1]}'", i + 1)
284â†’                            i += 2
285â†’                        }
286â†’                        'P' -> { i += 1 }
287â†’                        else -> parseException("Expected '+', '-', 'P', got '${text[i]}'", i)
288â†’                    }
289â†’                    state = AFTER_P
290â†’                    continue
291â†’                }
292â†’                var localSign = sign
293â†’                val iStart = i
294â†’                when (text[i]) {
295â†’                    '+', '-' -> {
296â†’                        if (text[i] == '-') localSign *= -1
297â†’                        i += 1
298â†’                        if (i >= text.length || text[i] !in '0'..'9')
299â†’                            parseException("A number expected after '${text[i]}'", i)
300â†’                    }
301â†’                    in '0'..'9' -> { }
302â†’                    'T' -> {
303â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_T)
304â†’                            parseException("Only one 'T' designator is allowed", i)
305â†’                        state = AFTER_T
306â†’                        i += 1
307â†’                        continue
308â†’                    }
309â†’                }
310â†’                var number = 0L
311â†’                while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9') {
312â†’                    try {
313â†’                        number = safeAdd(safeMultiply(number, 10), (text[i] - '0').toLong())
314â†’                    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
315â†’                        parseException("The number is too large", iStart)
316â†’                    }
317â†’                    i += 1
318â†’                }
319â†’                number *= localSign
320â†’                if (i == text.length)
321â†’                    parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
322â†’                val wrongOrder = "Wrong component order: should be 'Y', 'M', 'W', 'D', then designator 'T', then 'H', 'M', 'S'"
323â†’                fun Long.toIntThrowing(component: Char): Int {
324â†’                    if (this < Int.MIN_VALUE || this > Int.MAX_VALUE)
325â†’                        parseException("Value $this does not fit into an Int, which is required for component '$component'", iStart)
326â†’                    return toInt()
327â†’                }
328â†’                when (text[i].uppercaseChar()) {
329â†’                    'Y' -> {
330â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_YEAR)
331â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
332â†’                        state = AFTER_YEAR
333â†’                        years = number.toIntThrowing('Y')
334â†’                    }
335â†’                    'M' -> {
336â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_T) {
337â†’                            // Minutes
338â†’                            if (state >= AFTER_MINUTE)
339â†’                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
340â†’                            state = AFTER_MINUTE
341â†’                            minutes = number.toIntThrowing('M')
342â†’                        } else {
343â†’                            // Months
344â†’                            if (state >= AFTER_MONTH)
345â†’                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
346â†’                            state = AFTER_MONTH
347â†’                            months = number.toIntThrowing('M')
348â†’                        }
349â†’                    }
350â†’                    'W' -> {
351â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_WEEK)
352â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
353â†’                        state = AFTER_WEEK
354â†’                        weeks = number.toIntThrowing('W')
355â†’                    }
356â†’                    'D' -> {
357â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_DAY)
358â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
359â†’                        state = AFTER_DAY
360â†’                        days = number.toIntThrowing('D')
361â†’                    }
362â†’                    'H' -> {
363â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_HOUR || state < AFTER_T)
364â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
365â†’                        state = AFTER_HOUR
366â†’                        hours = number.toIntThrowing('H')
367â†’                    }
368â†’                    'S' -> {
369â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
370â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
371â†’                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
372â†’                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
373â†’                    }
374â†’                    '.', ',' -> {
375â†’                        i += 1
376â†’                        if (i >= text.length)
377â†’                            parseException("Expected designator 'S' after ${text[i - 1]}", i)
378â†’                        val iStartFraction = i
379â†’                        while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9')
380â†’                            i += 1
381â†’                        val fractionLength = i - iStartFraction
382â†’                        if (fractionLength > 9)
383â†’                            parseException("Only the nanosecond fractions of a second are supported", iStartFraction)
384â†’                        val fractionalPart = text.substring(iStartFraction, i) + "0".repeat(9 - fractionLength)
385â†’                        nanoseconds = fractionalPart.toInt(10) * localSign
386â†’                        if (text[i] != 'S')
387â†’                            parseException("Expected the 'S' designator after a fraction", i)
388â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
389â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
390â†’                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
391â†’                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
392â†’                    }
393â†’                    else -> parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
394â†’                }
395â†’                i += 1
396â†’           }
397â†’        }
398â†’    }
399â†’}
400â†’
401â†’/**
402â†’ * @suppress
403â†’ */
404â†’@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DateTimePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
405â†’public fun String.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = DateTimePeriod.parse(this)
406â†’
407â†’/**
408â†’ * A special case of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores the date components and has all time components equal to zero.
409â†’ *
410â†’ * A `DatePeriod` is automatically returned from all constructor functions for [DateTimePeriod] if it turns out that
411â†’ * the time components are zero.
412â†’ *
413â†’ * ```
414â†’ * DateTimePeriod.parse("P1Y3D") as DatePeriod // 1 year and 3 days
415â†’ * ```
416â†’ *
417â†’ * Additionally, [DatePeriod] has its own constructor, the [parse] function that will fail if any of the time components
418â†’ * are not zero, and [DatePeriodIso8601Serializer] and [DatePeriodComponentSerializer], mirroring those of
419â†’ * [DateTimePeriod].
420â†’ *
421â†’ * `DatePeriod` values are used in operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate] and are returned from operations
422â†’ * on [LocalDates][LocalDate], but they also can be passed anywhere a [DateTimePeriod] is expected.
423â†’ *
424â†’ * On the JVM, there are `DatePeriod.toJavaPeriod()` and `java.time.Period.toKotlinDatePeriod()`
425â†’ * extension functions to convert between `kotlinx.datetime` and `java.time` objects used for the same purpose.
426â†’ *
427â†’ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DatePeriodSamples.simpleParsingAndFormatting
428â†’ */
429â†’@Serializable(with = DatePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
430â†’public class DatePeriod internal constructor(
431â†’    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
432â†’    override val days: Int,
433â†’) : DateTimePeriod() {
434â†’    /**
435â†’     * Constructs a new [DatePeriod].
436â†’     *
437â†’     * It is recommended to always name the arguments explicitly when constructing this manually,
438â†’     * like `DatePeriod(years = 1, months = 12, days = 16)`.
439â†’     *
440â†’     * The passed numbers are not stored as is but are normalized instead for human readability, so, for example,
441â†’     * `DateTimePeriod(months = 24, days = 41)` becomes `DateTimePeriod(years = 2, days = 41)`.
442â†’     *
443â†’     * If only a single component is set and is always non-zero and is semantically a fixed time interval
444â†’     * (like "yearly" or "quarterly"), please consider using a multiple of [DateTimeUnit.DateBased] instead.
445â†’     * For example, instead of `DatePeriod(months = 6)`, one can use `DateTimeUnit.MONTH * 6`.
446â†’     *
447â†’     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [years] and [months] overflows an [Int].
448â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DatePeriodSamples.construction
449â†’     */
450â†’    public constructor(years: Int = 0, months: Int = 0, days: Int = 0): this(totalMonths(years, months), days)
451â†’    // avoiding excessive computations
452â†’    /** The number of whole hours in this period. Always equal to zero. */
453â†’    override val hours: Int get() = 0
454â†’
455â†’    /** The number of whole minutes in this period. Always equal to zero. */
456â†’    override val minutes: Int get() = 0
457â†’
458â†’    /** The number of whole seconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
459â†’    override val seconds: Int get() = 0
460â†’
461â†’    /** The number of nanoseconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
462â†’    override val nanoseconds: Int get() = 0
463â†’    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long get() = 0
464â†’
465â†’    public companion object {

493â†’    internal override val totalMonths: Int,

499â†’private fun totalMonths(years: Int, months: Int): Int =
500â†’    when (val totalMonths = years.toLong() * 12 + months.toLong()) {
501â†’        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> totalMonths.toInt()
502â†’        else -> throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of months in $years years and $months months overflows an Int")
503â†’    }
504â†’
505â†’private fun totalNanoseconds(hours: Int, minutes: Int, seconds: Int, nanoseconds: Long): Long {
506â†’    val totalMinutes: Long = hours.toLong() * 60 + minutes
507â†’    // absolute value at most 61 * Int.MAX_VALUE
508â†’    val totalMinutesAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutes * 60
509â†’    // absolute value at most 61 * 60 * Int.MAX_VALUE < 64 * 64 * 2^31 = 2^43
510â†’    val minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutesAsSeconds + nanoseconds / NANOS_PER_ONE
511â†’    // absolute value at most 2^43 + 2^63 / 10^9 < 2^43 + 2^34 < 2^44
512â†’    val totalSeconds = minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds + seconds
513â†’    // absolute value at most 2^44 + 2^31 < 2^45
514â†’    return try {
515â†’        multiplyAndAdd(totalSeconds, 1_000_000_000, nanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE)
516â†’    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
517â†’        throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of nanoseconds in $hours hours, $minutes minutes, $seconds seconds, and $nanoseconds nanoseconds overflows a Long")
518â†’    }
519â†’}
520â†’
521â†’internal fun buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths: Int = 0, days: Int = 0, totalNanoseconds: Long): DateTimePeriod =
522â†’    if (totalNanoseconds != 0L)

524â†’    else
525â†’        DatePeriod(totalMonths, days)

532â†’ *
533â†’ * The passed numbers are not stored as is but are normalized instead for human readability, so, for example,
534â†’ * `DateTimePeriod(months = 24, days = 41)` becomes `DateTimePeriod(years = 2, days = 41)`.
535â†’ *
536â†’ * If only a single component is set and is always non-zero and is semantically a fixed time interval
537â†’ * (like "yearly" or "quarterly"), please consider using a multiple of [DateTimeUnit] instead.
538â†’ * For example, instead of `DateTimePeriod(months = 6)`, one can use `DateTimeUnit.MONTH * 6`.
539â†’ *

content.text:
Now let me also verify that the `+P` and `-P` cases are handled by checking the existing `DateTimePeriod.parse` logic. When parsing `"+P"` or `"-P"`, after processing:
- `i=0`: sees `+`/`-`, checks `text[i+1] == 'P'` -> valid, i becomes 2
- `state = AFTER_P`
- `i >= text.length` (2 >= 2): true -> triggers `state == AFTER_P` check

Let me also verify that `"P0D"` still works, as it used to be the output from `toString()`:

Looking at the parse logic: For `"P0D"`:
- `i=0`: `'P'`, state=AFTER_P, i=1
- `i=1`: `'0'` is a digit, reads number=0, i=2
- `text[2]='D'`, state=AFTER_DAY, days=0, i=3
- `i=3 >= 3`: state is AFTER_DAY (not START, AFTER_P, or AFTER_T), so it returns.

This works correctly.

Now let me run the tests to make sure nothing is broken:

content.text:
The only failing tests are timezone-related (testZonedDateTime, testTimeZoneGreedyParsing, testByUnicodePatternDoc) - all pre-existing failures unrelated to our changes.

Let me now do a comprehensive summary of the changes I made:

## Summary of Changes

### 1. `DateTimePeriod.parse("P")` fix (`core/common/src/DateTimePeriod.kt`)
Added a check for `state == AFTER_P` in the end-of-input handler of the `parse` method. Previously, parsing just `"P"` (or `"+P"`, `"-P"`) without any date/time components would silently succeed and return a zero period. According to ISO 8601, at least one component is required after the `P` designator. Now it throws a `DateTimeFormatException` with a descriptive message.

### 2. `LocalDate.parse()` consistency fix (`core/jvm/src/LocalDate.kt` and `core/commonJs/src/LocalDate.kt`)
Removed the special-casing that delegated ISO format parsing to Java's `jtLocalDate.parse()` (or js-joda's equivalent). Now `LocalDate.parse(input)` always uses the `format.parse(input)` path, which is the same as `LocalDate.Formats.ISO.parse(input)`. This ensures both methods behave identically and can handle strings with many leading zeroes like `+00000000000002022-07-16` that Java's parser rejects.

### 3. `LocalDateTime.parse()` consistency fix (`core/jvm/src/LocalDateTime.kt` and `core/commonJs/src/LocalDateTime.kt`)
Same fix as #2 but for `LocalDateTime` - removed the delegation to Java/js-joda parsers and consistently uses the format-based parsing.

 85â†’@Deprecated("Use the plus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.plus(1, unit)"))
 86â†’public actual fun LocalDate.plus(unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate = plusNumber(1, unit)
 87â†’public actual fun LocalDate.plus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate = plusNumber(value, unit)
 88â†’public actual fun LocalDate.minus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate = plusNumber(-value, unit)

 91â†’private fun LocalDate.plusNumber(value: Number, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate =
 92â†’        try {
 93â†’            when (unit) {
 94â†’                is DateTimeUnit.DayBased -> jsTry { this.value.plusDays((value.toDouble() * unit.days).toInt()) }
 95â†’                is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased -> jsTry { this.value.plusMonths((value.toDouble() * unit.months).toInt()) }
 96â†’            }.let(::LocalDate)
 97â†’        } catch (e: Throwable) {
 98â†’            if (!e.isJodaDateTimeException() && !e.isJodaArithmeticException()) throw e
 99â†’            throw DateTimeArithmeticException("The result of adding $value of $unit to $this is out of LocalDate range.", e)
100â†’        }

103â†’public actual operator fun LocalDate.plus(period: DatePeriod): LocalDate = try {
104â†’    with(period) {
105â†’        return@with value
106â†’                .run { if (totalMonths != 0) jsTry { plusMonths(totalMonths) } else this }
107â†’                .run { if (days != 0) jsTry { plusDays(days) } else this }
108â†’
109â†’    }.let(::LocalDate)
110â†’} catch (e: Throwable) {
111â†’    if (e.isJodaDateTimeException() || e.isJodaArithmeticException()) throw DateTimeArithmeticException(e)
112â†’    throw e
113â†’}

117â†’public actual fun LocalDate.periodUntil(other: LocalDate): DatePeriod {
118â†’    var startD = this.value
119â†’    val endD = other.value
120â†’    val months = startD.until(endD, jtChronoUnit.MONTHS).toInt(); startD = jsTry { startD.plusMonths(months) }
121â†’    val days = startD.until(endD, jtChronoUnit.DAYS).toInt()
122â†’
123â†’    return DatePeriod(totalMonths = months, days)
124â†’}

126â†’public actual fun LocalDate.until(other: LocalDate, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): Int = when(unit) {
127â†’    is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased -> monthsUntil(other) / unit.months
128â†’    is DateTimeUnit.DayBased -> daysUntil(other) / unit.days
129â†’}

 17â†’/**
 18â†’ * A difference between two [instants][Instant], decomposed into date and time components.
 19â†’ *
 20â†’ * The date components are: [years] ([DateTimeUnit.YEAR]), [months] ([DateTimeUnit.MONTH]), and [days] ([DateTimeUnit.DAY]).
 21â†’ *
 22â†’ * The time components are: [hours] ([DateTimeUnit.HOUR]), [minutes] ([DateTimeUnit.MINUTE]),
 23â†’ * [seconds] ([DateTimeUnit.SECOND]), and [nanoseconds] ([DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND]).
 24â†’ *
 25â†’ * The time components are not independent and are always normalized together.
 26â†’ * Likewise, months are normalized together with years.
 27â†’ * For example, there is no difference between `DateTimePeriod(months = 24, hours = 2, minutes = 63)` and
 28â†’ * `DateTimePeriod(years = 2, hours = 3, minutes = 3)`.
 29â†’ *
 30â†’ * All components can also be negative: for example, `DateTimePeriod(months = -5, days = 6, hours = -3)`.
 31â†’ * Whereas `months = 5` means "5 months after," `months = -5` means "5 months earlier."
 32â†’ *
 33â†’ * A constant time interval that consists of a single non-zero component (like "yearly" or "quarterly") should be
 34â†’ * represented by a [DateTimeUnit] directly instead of a [DateTimePeriod]:
 35â†’ * for example, instead of `DateTimePeriod(months = 6)`, one could use `DateTimeUnit.MONTH * 6`.
 36â†’ * This provides a wider variety of operations: for example, finding how many such intervals fit between two instants
 37â†’ * or dates or adding a multiple of such intervals at once.
 38â†’ *
 39â†’ * ### Interaction with other entities
 40â†’ *
 41â†’ * [DateTimePeriod] can be returned from [Instant.periodUntil], representing the difference between two instants.
 42â†’ * Conversely, there is an [Instant.plus] overload that accepts a [DateTimePeriod] and returns a new instant.
 43â†’ *
 44â†’ * [DatePeriod] is a subtype of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores the date components and has all time components equal
 45â†’ * to zero.
 46â†’ *
 47â†’ * [DateTimePeriod] can be thought of as a combination of a [Duration] and a [DatePeriod], as it contains both the
 48â†’ * time components of [Duration] and the date components of [DatePeriod].
 49â†’ * [Duration.toDateTimePeriod] can be used to convert a [Duration] to the corresponding [DateTimePeriod].
 50â†’ *
 51â†’ * ### Construction, serialization, and deserialization
 52â†’ *
 53â†’ * When a [DateTimePeriod] is constructed in any way, a [DatePeriod] value, which is a subtype of [DateTimePeriod],
 54â†’ * will be returned if all time components happen to be zero.
 55â†’ *
 56â†’ * A `DateTimePeriod` can be constructed using the constructor function with the same name.
 57â†’ * See sample 1.
 58â†’ *
 59â†’ * [parse] and [toString] methods can be used to obtain a [DateTimePeriod] from and convert it to a string in the
 60â†’ * ISO 8601 extended format.
 61â†’ * See sample 2.
 62â†’ *
 63â†’ * `DateTimePeriod` can also be returned as the result of instant arithmetic operations (see [Instant.periodUntil]).
 64â†’ *
 65â†’ * Additionally, there are several `kotlinx-serialization` serializers for [DateTimePeriod]:
 66â†’ * - [DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer] for the ISO 8601 format;
 67â†’ * - [DateTimePeriodComponentSerializer]  for an object with components.
 68â†’ *
 69â†’ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.construction
 70â†’ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.simpleParsingAndFormatting
 71â†’ */
 72â†’@Serializable(with = DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
 73â†’// TODO: could be error-prone without explicitly named params
 74â†’public sealed class DateTimePeriod {
 75â†’    internal abstract val totalMonths: Int
 76â†’
 77â†’    /**
 78â†’     * The number of calendar days. Can be negative.
 79â†’     *
 80â†’     * Note that a calendar day is not identical to 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
 81â†’     * Also, this field does not get normalized together with months, so values larger than 31 can be present.
 82â†’     *
 83â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 84â†’     */
 85â†’    public abstract val days: Int
 86â†’    internal abstract val totalNanoseconds: Long
 87â†’
 88â†’    /**
 89â†’     * The number of whole years. Can be negative.
 90â†’     *
 91â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 92â†’     */
 93â†’    public val years: Int get() = totalMonths / 12
 94â†’
 95â†’    /**
 96â†’     * The number of months in this period that don't form a whole year, so this value is always in `(-11..11)`.
 97â†’     *
 98â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 99â†’     */
100â†’    public val months: Int get() = totalMonths % 12
101â†’
102â†’    /**
103â†’     * The number of whole hours in this period. Can be negative.
104â†’     *
105â†’     * This field does not get normalized together with days, so values larger than 23 or smaller than -23 can be present.
106â†’     *
107â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
108â†’     */
109â†’    public open val hours: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds / 3_600_000_000_000).toInt()
110â†’
111â†’    /**
112â†’     * The number of whole minutes in this period that don't form a whole hour, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
113â†’     *
114â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
115â†’     */
116â†’    public open val minutes: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 3_600_000_000_000) / 60_000_000_000).toInt()
117â†’
118â†’    /**
119â†’     * The number of whole seconds in this period that don't form a whole minute, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
120â†’     *
121â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
122â†’     */
123â†’    public open val seconds: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 60_000_000_000) / NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
124â†’
125â†’    /**
126â†’     * The number of whole nanoseconds in this period that don't form a whole second, so this value is always in
127â†’     * `(-999_999_999..999_999_999)`.
128â†’     *
129â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
130â†’     */
131â†’    public open val nanoseconds: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
132â†’
133â†’    private fun allNonpositive() =
134â†’        totalMonths <= 0 && days <= 0 && totalNanoseconds <= 0 && (totalMonths or days != 0 || totalNanoseconds != 0L)
135â†’
136â†’    /**
137â†’     * Converts this period to the ISO 8601 string representation for durations, for example, `P2M1DT3H`.
138â†’     *
139â†’     * Note that the ISO 8601 duration is not the same as [Duration],
140â†’     * but instead includes the date components, like [DateTimePeriod] does.
141â†’     *
142â†’     * Examples of the output:
143â†’     * - `P2Y4M-1D`: two years, four months, minus one day;
144â†’     * - `-P2Y4M1D`: minus two years, minus four months, minus one day;
145â†’     * - `P1DT3H2M4.123456789S`: one day, three hours, two minutes, four seconds, 123456789 nanoseconds;
146â†’     * - `P1DT-3H-2M-4.123456789S`: one day, minus three hours, minus two minutes,
147â†’     *   minus four seconds, minus 123456789 nanoseconds;
148â†’     *
149â†’     * See ISO-8601-1:2019, 5.5.2.2a)
150â†’     *
151â†’     * @see DateTimePeriod.parse for the detailed description of the format.
152â†’     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.toStringSample
153â†’     */
154â†’    override fun toString(): String = buildString {
155â†’        val sign = if (allNonpositive()) { append('-'); -1 } else 1
156â†’        append('P')
157â†’        if (years != 0) append(years * sign).append('Y')
158â†’        if (months != 0) append(months * sign).append('M')
159â†’        if (days != 0) append(days * sign).append('D')
160â†’        var t = "T"
161â†’        if (hours != 0) append(t).append(hours * sign).append('H').also { t = "" }
162â†’        if (minutes != 0) append(t).append(minutes * sign).append('M').also { t = "" }
163â†’        if (seconds or nanoseconds != 0) {
164â†’            append(t)
165â†’            append(when {
166â†’                seconds != 0 -> seconds * sign
167â†’                nanoseconds * sign < 0 -> "-0"
168â†’                else -> "0"
169â†’            })
170â†’            if (nanoseconds != 0) append('.').append((nanoseconds.absoluteValue).toString().padStart(9, '0'))
171â†’            append('S')
172â†’        }
173â†’
174â†’        if (length == 1) append("0D")
175â†’    }
176â†’
177â†’    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
178â†’        if (this === other) return true
179â†’        if (other !is DateTimePeriod) return false
180â†’
181â†’        if (totalMonths != other.totalMonths) return false
182â†’        if (days != other.days) return false
183â†’        if (totalNanoseconds != other.totalNanoseconds) return false
184â†’
185â†’        return true
186â†’    }
187â†’
188â†’    override fun hashCode(): Int {
189â†’        var result = totalMonths
190â†’        result = 31 * result + days
191â†’        result = 31 * result + totalNanoseconds.hashCode()
192â†’        return result
193â†’    }
